original_abstract: Quality higher education serves as a fundamental pillar for a nation’s socio-economic advancement, with service quality acting as a primary metric for institutional success. This study examines the persistent challenges facing the Ethiopian higher education sector through a qualitative research lens. Data were gathered through a comprehensive analysis of 89 research papers, government policy documents, peer-reviewed articles, and doctoral dissertations, supplemented by semi-structured interviews and focus group discussions. The findings indicate that despite the implementation of various quality assurance frameworks, these initiatives have had a negligible impact on overall standards. Critical systemic deficiencies remain, including a shortage of experienced academic staff, inadequate ICT infrastructure, and subpar library and laboratory facilities. Furthermore, students continue to face poor living conditions and overcrowded classrooms. The study concludes that the majority of Ethiopian higher education institutions struggle with substandard service delivery, hindering their academic mission. Based on these insights, the paper offers strategic recommendations to address these infrastructure and personnel gaps.
humanized_abstract: This research investigates the challenges prevailing in the higher education sector of Ethiopia in qualitative terms. A total of 89 articles, government policy documents, journals and PhD researches are analyzed, in addition to a set of interviews and focus group discussions that were conducted with different stakeholders in higher education institutions of Ethiopia. After thoroughly assessing the quality assurance initiatives established to develop higher education institutions and ensure high quality learning and teaching processes for students, it was found out that the establishments did not lead to any significant improvement in the overall performance of higher education sector in Ethiopia. Lack of adequate number of professionals in higher education institutions, improper utilization of information and communication technology, insufficient development of library and laboratory services and students’ inadequate living standards due to over crowded hostels are the key structural challenges in the higher education sector of Ethiopia. Moreover, it was also revealed that the majority of higher education institutions in Ethiopia provide low quality services below standard values which negatively affect their academic goals. Recommendations for measures to mitigate structural obstacles, infrastructure and human resource weaknesses are provided in this study.
